role_summary: AFSCME (American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees) International Secretary-Treasurer, 1972 to 2010; co-founder and first president of the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists; co-founder of the Free South Africa Movement.

photo.alt: William Lucy at the Eugene V. Debs Dinner in Chicago, 1984
photo.caption: William Lucy at the Eugene V. Debs Foundation dinner in Chicago, 1984, during his tenure as AFSCME International Secretary-Treasurer.
photo.photographer: Unidentified (released into the public domain by the source)

ai_reading: The cosine-similarity neighborhood around Lucy's testimony in this corpus is led by Kay Tillow (entry 74) and Walter Tillow (entry 124), two later-career labor-and-health organizers, by a wide margin: the aggregated overlap count to Kay Tillow is the single highest of any neighbor and roughly the size of his next two neighbors combined. The labor vocabulary, collective-bargaining, the worker as a party to the decision, the floor under wages, seems to dominate his vector signature over the Memphis-1968 material that the cultural record foregrounds. What the embedding does not surface is also notable: where a textbook account would tie Lucy first to the sanitation strike's SCLC orbit, his nearest neighbor with a catalog page on that side, Wyatt Tee Walker (entry 132), sits below not only the two Tillows but also William Saunders (entry 130), a South Carolina labor and community organizer, and Judge D'Army Bailey (entry 26), the Memphis civil-rights attorney. The space reads Lucy as a labor organizer who passed through Memphis, not as a Memphis figure who happened to be in a union.
biographical_paragraph: William Lucy was born in Memphis on November 26, 1933, and raised in Richmond, California, where his father had gone to weld in the Kaiser shipyards during the Second World War [src: 2, 3]. He worked for Contra Costa County, California as a materials and research engineer for thirteen years before joining the national staff of AFSCME in 1966 as associate director of legislation and community affairs [src: 2, 3]. In February 1968 the union sent him to his birth city to assess a walkout by roughly 1,300 Memphis sanitation workers, and he stayed to help direct it; with a local minister he reduced the strikers' grievance to the placard slogan I Am a Man, which he traces in his oral history to Rev. James M. Lawson Jr's definition of racism as treating a man as though he were not one [src: 2, 4]. He became AFSCME's International Secretary-Treasurer in 1972 and held the office until 2010 [src: 1, 2]. The same year he co-founded the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists, with Nelson Jack Edwards, Charles A. Hayes, Cleveland Robinson, and William H. Simons, and served as its first president [src: 2, 5]. In the 1980s he co-founded the Free South Africa Movement, and in 1994 he was elected president of Public Services International, the first African American to lead that global labor federation [src: 2, 4]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 128 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: William Lucy
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked how the Memphis walkout began, Lucy distinguished the cause of a strike from the mood of the workers:
interview_snippets.quote: Strikes don't come because workers are mad. Strikes become because they're frustrated and have no ability to resolve the issues that contribute to the frustration.

interview_snippets.speaker: William Lucy
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the imbalance of power that defined the sanitation strike:
interview_snippets.quote: It was a classic confrontation between a city with total power and a group of workers with no power other than a good idea.

interview_snippets.speaker: William Lucy
interview_snippets.lead_in: On what the strikers were actually demanding, beyond wages and safety:
interview_snippets.quote: It was the lack of respect, the absence of dignity, that caused these workers to say, we just had enough.

interview_snippets.speaker: William Lucy
interview_snippets.lead_in: Lucy describes settling on the strike's slogan after working through wording at the Peabody Hotel:
interview_snippets.quote: And it simply said, I am a man.

interview_snippets.speaker: William Lucy
interview_snippets.lead_in: On naming the new labor organization founded after the 1972 election:
interview_snippets.quote: And we intentionally called it Coalition of Black Trade Unionists.
